teamObjective.explanation: With only two cards left while opponents hold three each, the priority is to empty my hand as quickly as possible by retaining the lead through both plays.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner has three cards and has made no public plays, so there is no reliable signal about their strength or suit distribution. They may be able to cover if I lose the lead, but no specific inference is warranted.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents each hold three cards and will try to block me from going out. They likely possess some trump-ranked cards or wildcards that can beat non-trump singles, especially since trump rank is 7 and no trumps or bombs have been played yet.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 2 (the higher of my two non-trump singles) maximizes the chance of winning this trick immediately so I can lead the A next and finish my hand. If the 2 is beaten, I still preserve the slightly weaker A as a potential recovery card.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The A is lower than the 2, so it is more likely to be beaten by an opponent's higher card or a partner cover. If I lead the A and lose the lead, I am left with only the 2 and may never regain control to play it.

risks: An opponent or partner may play a trump, wildcard, or another 2 to take the lead, leaving me with one card and no guaranteed way to play it.
Even if I win this trick, the next lead (A) could be beaten before I go out.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Leading the strongest available single is the best available play. It gives the highest probability of winning the current trick and continuing, and any loss of lead must be covered by partner or accepted as hidden-card uncertainty.
